senses.definition: to force someone to work hard or endure harsh, exhausting labor, especially under coercion.
examples: The prisoners were slaving away at forced labor for long hours.
She felt she was slaving over the project with little reward.

senses.definition: showing a very strong, almost servile obedience or dedication; like a slave in manner or attitude.
examples: He gave a slavish devotion to his employer.
The critic dismissed the slavish imitation as uninspired.

senses.definition: in a way that shows extreme or unquestioning obedience or imitation.
examples: She followed the rules slavishly.
He copied the boss’s style slavishly.

senses.definition: the quality of being excessively willing to obey or imitate; extreme servility.
examples: The slavishness of his compliance angered his colleagues.
Her slavishness to tradition limited innovation.

senses.definition: plural of slavishness; multiple instances of extreme servility or conformity.
examples: The text critiques the slavishnesses of corporate culture.
They rejected the slavishnesses imposed by outdated norms.

senses.definition: a movement or ideology that emphasizes loyalty to Slavic cultures; the belief in the political or cultural unity of Slavic peoples.
examples: Scholars debated the implications of slavism in early European politics.
The book traces the rise of slavism and its influence on regional alliances.

senses.definition: a person who studies Slavic languages, literature, or cultures; a scholar of Slavic studies.
examples: The conference featured several renowned slavists from Europe.
As a slavist, she specializes in Cyrillic languages.

senses.definition: relating to Slavic languages or the study of Slavic languages.
examples: Slavistic research includes phonology and syntax of Russian and Polish.
She contributed Slavistic insights to the journal.

senses.definition: the process of becoming or making something Slavic in character or influence; incorporation into Slavic culture or political life.
examples: The slavization of the region altered its linguistic landscape.
Policies aimed at slavization shaped local education.

senses.definition: to make or become Slavic in culture, language, or influence.
examples: The reform aimed to slavize regional rituals into a common identity.
Efforts to slavize education included introducing Slavic literature.

senses.definition: governments in which Slavic-speaking elites hold power; states with strong Slavic influence or control.
examples: Historians debated the nature of the regional slavocracies in medieval times.
The alliance shifted when several slavocracies formed a bloc.

senses.definition: rule or government by Slavic people; a system dominated by Slavic authorities.
examples: Some researchers argued that slavocracy suppressed non-Slavic voices.
The term slavocracy appeared in debates about regional governance.

senses.definition: a person who supports or participates in slavocracy; a Slavic ruler or strong advocate of Slavic governance.
examples: The historian identified the figure as a slavocrat in the late medieval court.
Slavocrats argued for protective policies for Slavic nations.

senses.definition: relating to slavocracy or Slavic governance; characteristic of a Slavocratic system.
examples: The slavocratic regime faced internal resistance.
Slavocratic policies influenced regional trade.

senses.definition: plural of slavocrat; individuals who advocate or support slavocracy.
examples: The slavocrats faced criticism from reform-minded groups.
Historically, slavocrats allied with other regional powers.

senses.definition: an ethnolinguistic group or person from Slavic or Slavic-influenced regions; historically used as a demonym or nationality term.
examples: The scholars discussed Slavic groups, including Slavonians.
Literature from Slavonian writers is studied in class.

senses.definition: to make something Slavonian in character or influence; to assimilate into Slavonian culture.
examples: Efforts were made to slavonianize the region’s schools.
The festival Slavonianized local traditions with Slavic motifs.

senses.definition: relating to Slavic languages or peoples; used in older or more formal academic contexts.
examples: Slavonic languages include Russian, Polish, and Czech.
They studied Slavonic literature in the graduate program.

senses.definition: the Slavonic languages collectively; sometimes used to refer to groups of Slavic peoples.
examples: The instructor taught several Slavonic excerpts from the 18th century.
Conversations covered key features of Slavonic grammar.

senses.definition: in a Slavonic or Slavic manner, or in a way characteristic of Slavonic languages or culture.
examples: The text was written slavonically, echoing older Slavic literary traditions.
She spoke slavonically with a cadence typical of the region.

senses.definition: to render in Slavonic style or to adopt Slavonic elements; to make more Slavonic in character.
examples: The museum exhibit Slavonicized several artifacts with Slavic motifs.
Policy changes Slavonicize the cultural program.

senses.definition: a jocular or informal/rare term referring to Slavic-related traits or people; used infrequently.
examples: The mascot included a playful nod to slavonish culture.
A slavic-inspired accent gave the performance a slavonish flair.

senses.definition: a Slavonic or Slavic linguistic, cultural, or political feature; a doctrine or belief relating to Slavic peoples.
examples: Scholars debated the validity of certain slavonisms in modern text.

senses.definition: the process of Slavonizing; the act or policy of making something Slavonic in character.
examples: Slavonization of the curriculum faced criticism from minority groups.
The report examined the effects of slavonization on regional identity.

senses.definition: to make Slavonic in character or to adopt Slavonic elements; to Slavicize.
examples: There were attempts to slavonize regional traditions.
The curriculum was slavonized to include more Slavic literature.

senses.definition: a person who strongly approves or loves Slavic culture and traditions.
examples: The slavophil spoke proudly of Slavic heritage.
As a slavophil, he supported initiatives to preserve Slavic arts.
